title: Weeping by Night
subtitle: Women's Love, Marriage, and Resistance in Greco-Roman Egypt

sections.title: The Line That Survived
sections.body_html:
Weeping by night and lamenting by day.1

He wrote that about himself. A husband, unwashed for a month, scribbling his grief on papyrus in a provincial Egyptian city. The phrase has survived nineteen centuries because someone dropped the letter in the trash and the desert kept it. But the woman he was weeping for — Isidora, who had walked out — left no letter of her own. Her side of the story lives only in the distortions of his.1

This is a history of women who defied their husbands. It is told, almost entirely, in the words of men.

sections.title: A Letter from the Rubbish Heap
sections.body_html:
The line comes from P.Oxy. 3.528, a discarded letter found in the rubbish heaps of Oxyrhynchus — modern el-Bahnasa in Middle Egypt — by Bernard Grenfell and Arthur Hunt in the late 1890s.2 By the time Serenus wrote it, probably in the second century CE, Oxyrhynchus was a crowded provincial capital under Roman rule, full of baths, temples, offices, and notaries, a city of perhaps thirty thousand people where a broken marriage could still be compressed into a single sheet of papyrus.3

The full text, translated from the Greek:

Serenus to his beloved sister Isidora, many greetings. Before all else I pray for your health, and every day and evening I perform the act of veneration on your behalf to Thoeris who loves you. I assure you that ever since you left me I have been in mourning, weeping by night and lamenting by day. Since we bathed together on Phaophi 12, I never bathed nor anointed myself until Athur 12. You sent me letters which would have shaken a stone, so much did your words move me. Instantly I answered you and gave the letter sealed to the messenger on the 12th, together with letters for you. Apart from your saying and writing “Colobus has made me a prostitute,” he said to me, “Your wife sent me a message saying ‘He himself has sold the chain and himself put me in the boat.’” You say this to prevent my being believed any longer with regard to my embarkation. See how many times I have sent to you! Whether you are coming or not, let me know.4

That is everything. A single page. No sequel, no reply, no resolution. Perhaps Serenus never sent it. Perhaps he sent it and got nothing back. Perhaps he drafted it, sealed it, broke the seal, and threw it away. However it happened, the sheet wound up in a dump outside the city, where the dry Egyptian ground did what neither spouse intended: it kept the quarrel.

Serenus addresses Isidora as “his beloved sister.”5 In Egyptian letters that could be a wife’s title of affection, but it might also mean exactly what it says. In Roman Egypt, sibling marriage among non-citizens was not a lurid exception but a documented practice, common enough in the census returns to surprise modern readers and specific enough to trouble Roman administrators, who forbade such unions to Romans while tolerating them among Egyptians.6 The Gnomon of the Idios Logos, that dry handbook of fiscal rules and confiscations, spells the distinction out with bureaucratic coldness: Romans could not marry their sisters; others might, depending on status and circumstance.7 In Egypt, kinship, taxation, and legality were tangled together so tightly that even the language of endearment could also be a legal clue.8

Whether Serenus and Isidora were truly brother and sister disappears into the paper’s silence. What survives is the intimacy: the form of address, the shared bath, the tone of a man writing as if half his household had been torn away.

Their names are ordinary ones. Serenus bears a Latin name common in Roman Egypt.9 Isidora, “gift of Isis,” was one of the most familiar women’s names in the papyri.10 Even the intermediary called Colobus carried the sort of blunt physical nickname that stuck in small communities.11 Nothing about the cast is grand. The grandeur comes only from accident: someone saved the paper by throwing it away.

sections.title: What Happened Between Them
sections.body_html:
Serenus has not bathed for thirty days.

In a place like Oxyrhynchus, where public baths were part of the city’s ordinary machinery, that was not a small detail but a visible one.12 Washing and anointing were paired habits, the sort of routine that marked a person as presentable, social, still participating in the life of the town.13 To stop both for a month was to let grief show on the skin.

It was also a recognized form of mourning. In Egyptian and Greek funerary practice, mourners abstained from bathing, from anointing, from cutting their hair. Serenus is performing the rituals of bereavement for a wife who is not dead but gone.14 The precision of his accounting — “since we bathed together on Phaophi 12, I never bathed nor anointed myself until Athur 12” — suggests not merely grief but the counting of days, the obsessive tracking of absence. Phaophi 12 to Athur 12: exactly one month in the Egyptian calendar.15 The last shared act of the marriage was the most intimate and the most mundane: they washed together. Then she left.

The shared bath matters for another reason. It is the one ordinary domestic image in the whole letter. For a moment the marriage comes into focus not as a lawsuit in waiting but as two people washing together, close enough to leave steam, oil, and memory behind. Everything after that belongs to the separation.

The central knot of the letter is the accusation involving Colobus.

Isidora has apparently said or written: “Colobus has made me a prostitute.”16 There is no soft way to translate it. She is accusing someone — Colobus, or Serenus through Colobus — of pushing her into sexual degradation for profit.

But the accusation runs both ways. Colobus, Serenus says, brought back a message from Isidora: Serenus had sold her chain and put her in a boat.17 A chain was not a trinket. In Egyptian marriage documents, gold chains, earrings, and necklaces sit at the center of the dowry lists because they were portable wealth, the kind of property a woman could point to, weigh, value, and demand back.18 Other Oxyrhynchite contracts list such jewelry item by item, stone by stone.1920 If Serenus sold Isidora’s chain, he sold part of the financial protection the marriage was supposed to preserve.

And then there is the boat.21 In Egypt a boat was road, cart, and prison cell all at once: the thing that took grain to market, tax revenue to the state, and unwilling people away from home. Was Isidora saying she had been shipped off? Expelled? Trafficked? The broken wording will not let us settle it.22 What it does preserve is the shape of her charge: money taken, movement forced, body endangered.

What we can say is that both parties are hurling accusations of sexual and economic exploitation at each other through intermediaries, and that the intermediary — Colobus — is playing both sides. This is not a clean separation. This is a marriage in which the fundamental question — who exploited whom? — remains unresolved across nineteen centuries.

Isidora did not leave us a surviving letter. She did, however, leave Serenus trembling before one. “You sent me letters which would have shaken a stone,” he says.23 Somewhere in Oxyrhynchus there was once another sheet of papyrus, perhaps several, carrying her version in a hand we do not have. Perhaps she wrote them herself. Perhaps she dictated them to a scribe in a hot office that smelled of ink and old reeds. What survives is their effect: she could write strongly enough to make him tell on himself.

sections.title: Other Ways a Marriage Broke
sections.body_html:
What do we know about why women left their husbands in Roman Egypt?

The papyri record many reasons. Syra, daughter of Theon, petitioning the archidikastes sometime between 20 and 50 CE, accused her husband Sarapion of squandering her dowry, ill-treating and insulting her, using violence, depriving her of necessaries, and finally deserting her.24 Senyris, petitioning the chief judge around 156-125 BCE, reported that her partner “threw me out and he doesn’t even feed or clothe us. And on top of that, he has taken on another woman.”25 Makaria, petitioning the strategos after 55 BCE, wrote that her husband “threw me out of the house, and he was not satisfied even with this, nor did he provide me with any necessities or clothing. Nor has he made restitution of the dowry.”26 In the sixth century CE, a father named John dissolved his daughter Euphemia’s engagement to Phoebammon on the grounds that Phoebammon was “giving yourself over to lawless deeds, which are pleasing to neither God nor man, and are not fit to be put into writing.”27

The pattern is consistent across centuries. Women left — or were thrown out — because of violence, neglect, financial exploitation, sexual betrayal, and generalized “lawless deeds.” The vocabulary changed. The complaints did not.

Isidora fits that long pattern. She accused Serenus of selling her chain, of exposing her to sexual ruin, of forcing her out into the current of the Nile world. She left. She wrote back hard enough to frighten him. And then the trail goes dark. Did she return? Did she remarry? Did she simply refuse him the dignity of another answer? The rubbish mound keeps his question and swallows hers.

sections.title: The Wife Who Took the Pillow
sections.body_html:
A century later, another woman left another husband. This time the husband wrote not a love letter but a petition.

P.Heid. 3.237, dated to the mid-third century CE, is a petition from Panetbeous, a public farmer from the village of Theadelphia in the Arsinoite nome (the Fayum), addressed to the centurion Claudius Alexandros.28 The full text:

My cohabiting wife, from whom I also had children, having conceived hostile intentions toward our life together, took advantage of my absence and left my house some months ago without the so-called formal divorce, having carried off both her own things and very many of ours, among which was a large white undressed garment, an Oxyrhynchite pillow, a cross-striped double-blanket, materials for two tunics, and other agricultural tools. And although I sent to her many times, wishing to recover our property, she, being unrepentant, returned nothing — despite the fact that I provided her with the maintenance allowance for the child. Since then I have now learned that a certain Neilos son of Syros from the same village has unlawfully seized and married her, I submit this petition and request that she and Neilos be brought before you, so that I may be able to obtain justice and recover our property.29

Panetbeous does not give her a name. He calls her his cohabiting wife, the woman who had shared his house and borne his children.30 The petition reduces her to a legal relation and then to a problem. Still, the list of what she carried off lets her step out of his wording for a moment and become practical, deliberate, almost visible.

A large white garment, still unfinished. Cloth that could be worn, cut, traded, or remade.31

An Oxyrhynchite pillow, the kind known by its city of manufacture, good enough that the origin mattered.32

A double blanket with colored stripes, thick enough to count as wealth in a farming house.33

Materials for two tunics: thread, cloth, work in waiting.

And agricultural tools besides. Not just bedding and cloth, but the tools by which the house fed itself.

This was not an impulsive flight. A woman who takes a pillow, a blanket, raw cloth, sewing materials, and agricultural tools is planning for self-sufficiency. She took what she needed to survive independently: something to sleep on, something to cover herself with, something to sell or make into clothes, and tools that could be worked or traded.34 The inventory is a portrait of calculated departure — a woman who understood the material basis of survival and stripped the household of exactly what she required.

Panetbeous sees theft. But another reading is possible: she took her own things — ta idía — and “very many of ours” — kai tōn hēmetérōn pleîsta. The line between “hers” and “theirs” was the line that marriage contracts were designed to draw, and that separations routinely erased. Without a formal marriage contract specifying what belonged to whom, the distinction depended on memory, assertion, and power. She had departed with advantage. He had the pen.

Panetbeous adds a detail that he clearly intends as proof of his decency: he says he kept paying support for the child.35

That mattered. Egyptian marriage law and divorce practice repeatedly treated child support and birth expenses as obligations that had to be recorded, valued, and enforced.3637 Panetbeous wants the centurion to see him as the man who obeyed the paperwork even after the household broke.

Panetbeous is telling the centurion that he fulfilled this obligation. He paid the child support. He played by the rules. And still she did not return his property. The phrase “being unrepentant” — ametamelētos — carries a note of moral outrage. She was not merely absent; she was defiant. She had been sent to multiple times and had refused to budge.

Then the petition swerves into melodrama. Panetbeous says he has learned that a man named Neilos, from the same village, “seized and married her.”38

The wording matters. One verb makes the event sound like abduction; the other makes it sound like settled domestic life. Panetbeous needs both. If she left him willingly for another man, his case narrows into an ugly property dispute. If she was “seized,” then he becomes the wronged husband of a public outrage.39

Panetbeous has every reason to frame the situation as abduction. If his wife voluntarily left him for another man, his legal position is weaker — she chose to leave, she chose Neilos, the property dispute is between estranged spouses. But if Neilos “seized” her, then Neilos is a criminal, the wife is a victim (or at least a passive object), and Panetbeous is the wronged party twice over: robbed of his goods and then of his wife.39

But consider the sequence. The wife left Panetbeous “some months ago.” She took household goods and agricultural tools. She refused, repeatedly, to return them. She was “unrepentant.” And then — “since then” — she ended up with Neilos. This is not the chronology of a sudden abduction. This is the chronology of a woman who left one man, established herself, and then joined another. The “seizing” may describe a formal act of marriage — the word harpázein could refer to the traditional wedding ritual in which the groom symbolically “carried off” the bride — rather than an act of violence.40

The paper does not let us see her face, only the trail of her decisions: the packed goods, the repeated refusals, the new household. That trail points more toward choice than passivity. She reads less like a captive than like a woman who timed her exit well.

sections.title: What Marriage Was Supposed to Guarantee
sections.body_html:
To understand what women gained and risked in marriage, we must read the contracts.

The oldest complete Greek marriage contract from Egypt, from the island of Elephantine, dates to 310 BCE. It is the marriage of Herakleides the Temnitan and Demetria the Koan — two Greek settlers in the earliest years of Ptolemaic rule:

Herakleides takes as his lawful wife Demetria the Koan, a free man a free woman, from her father Leptines, Koan, and her mother Philotis, bringing clothing and ornaments to the value of 1000 drachmas. […] If Demetria is discovered doing any evil to the shame of her husband Herakleides, she is to be deprived of all that she brought. […] It shall not be permitted for Herakleides to bring home another wife in insult of Demetria or to have children by another woman or to do evil against Demetria on any pretext. If Herakleides is discovered doing any of these things and Demetria proves it before three men whom they both approve, Herakleides shall give back to Demetria the dowry of 1000 drachmas which she brought and shall moreover forfeit 1000 drachmas of the silver coinage of Alexander.41

The symmetry is striking. If she shames him, she loses her dowry. If he shames her, he pays double. Both sides face arbitration by three mutually approved judges. The language is contractual, not sentimental; the marriage is an economic arrangement protected by penalties. But the penalties are not equal. The wife’s infidelity costs her 1,000 drachmae (her dowry). The husband’s infidelity costs him 2,000 (her dowry returned plus an equal fine). The husband’s faithlessness is priced twice as high — not because women’s feelings mattered more, but because a wife’s economic security depended entirely on the marriage. Losing the dowry meant destitution. The doubled penalty was a deterrent calibrated to the power imbalance.

This contract set the template for centuries. Nearly every subsequent marriage contract from Greco-Roman Egypt contains variations on the same clauses: the dowry, the prohibition against mistreatment, the provision for return on separation, the penalty for default.

The dowry was not a payment to the husband. It was the wife’s property, held in trust by the husband during the marriage, returnable on separation or death.42 But the composition of the dowry reveals the material world of these marriages with extraordinary precision.

At the lower end: Tryphon, a weaver of Oxyrhynchus, received from his second wife Saraeus in 37 CE a dowry of 72 drachmae total — 40 in cash, a pair of gold earrings valued at 20 drachmae, and a milk-white robe worth 12 drachmae. The marriage existed without a formal contract; the dowry was registered as a receipt.43

In the middle range: Chaeremon, a man identified as “a Persian of the Epigone” (a conventional legal status designation that by this date said nothing about actual ethnicity) received from his father-in-law Sisois in 66 CE, as dowry upon his daughter Thaisarion, a substantial collection: 100 drachmae in silver, a pair of gold ear-rings weighing four quarters, a gold crescent of three quarters, two gold rings of two quarters, a pair of silver armlets weighing 44 drachmae of unstamped metal, two bracelets weighing 16 drachmae, two robes (one white, one narcissus-colored), five mantles, bronze utensils and a bath weighing four minae, two bronze vessels, and five minae of tin. Plus ten and three-quarter arourae of catoecic land, granted in usufruct as a gift.44 This was a prosperous family: land, gold, silver, bronze, clothing, a bathtub.

At the upper end: Aurelia Thaesis, daughter of Eudaemon and Herais, gave her daughter Aurelia Tausiris in marriage in 260 CE with a dowry of extraordinary richness: a gold necklace called a maniaces with a stone weighing 13 quarters, a brooch with five stones set in gold weighing 4 quarters, a pair of ear-rings with ten pearls weighing 3 quarters, a small ring of 4 quarters, a silvery striped Dalmatian veil worth 260 drachmae, a white tasselled frock worth 160 drachmae, a turquoise Dalmatian veil worth 100 drachmae, and a white Dalmatian veil with a purple border worth 100 drachmae. Total: one mina and four and a half quarters of gold, plus 620 drachmae in clothing.45

And at the very top: Aurelia Aristous, daughter of the ex-gymnasiarch and senator Aurelius Herodes son of Apion, declared upon her husband Achillion’s death that her dowry of gold ornaments, clothing, and other objects was valued at two talents and 3,000 drachmae of silver — the equivalent of 15,000 silver drachmae.46 This was elite wealth. The estate itself was registered at 200,000 sesterces.

Now consider the wife of Panetbeous, taking from the house a white garment, a pillow, a blanket, sewing materials, and farm tools. She was not taking a gold necklace or a pair of pearl earrings. She was taking what she could carry and what she could use. The gap between Aurelia Tausiris’s bejeweled dowry and the unnamed wife’s bundle of textiles measures the distance between Oxyrhynchus’s gymnasiarchal elite and the farming villages of the Fayum. Both women’s possessions are documented with the same bureaucratic precision. Both women were embedded in the same legal system. But their material lives were as different as silk and sackcloth.

sections.title: Leaving on Paper, Leaving in Practice
sections.body_html:
Could a woman legally leave her husband in Roman Egypt? On paper, yes — more easily than in many other ancient societies.

The divorce law of Ptolemaic Ptolemais Hermiu, preserved on a first-century inscription, made the procedure straightforward: “When anyone sends away his wife or leaves her, he shall register with the thesmophylakes his own name and that of his father, the month and the day. Let the wife also register in the same manner, and likewise the father, if he is still living.”47 Either party could initiate. Both were required to register. The language treats husband and wife as procedurally equal.

Marriage contracts reinforce this equality. The contract of Chaeremon and Thaisarion in 66 CE provides for separation “either Chaeremon divorcing Thaisarion or Thaisarion withdrawing of her own free will.”48 The contract of 260 CE provides for separation “if — which heaven forbid — in consequence of an estrangement a separation of the parties takes place.”49 The contract of Apollonius and Thatres in 170 CE provides that “if a separation takes place after the birth of children or before it, the husband shall restore all the super-dowry at the time of separation.”50 No contract makes divorce the exclusive right of the husband.

In practice, however, the right to leave was constrained by economic reality. A woman who divorced lost her daily maintenance — the food, clothing, and shelter that marriage contracts required husbands to provide “in proportion to his means.”51 She gained back her dowry — but only if the husband had it and was willing to return it. The dowry return clause was the most frequently litigated provision in Egyptian family law. Syra petitioned the archidikastes because Sarapion had squandered her 200 drachmae. Makaria petitioned the strategos because her husband “threw me out of the house […] nor did he provide me with any necessities or clothing. Nor has he made restitution of the dowry.”52 The right to leave was real. The right to leave with your property was often theoretical.

This is the context for the wife of Panetbeous’s departure. She left “without the so-called formal divorce” — aneu tēs legomenēs katà nómon apoluseos.53 She did not register. She did not go through the thesmophylakes or whatever office handled divorce registration in mid-third-century Theadelphia. She simply left, taking with her what she deemed necessary. The formal divorce procedure existed to protect property rights. By skipping it, she gained the advantage of surprise and the first claim on the household goods. By the time Panetbeous discovered the absence, the pillow, the blanket, and the farm tools were already gone.

There was another constraint on women’s legal autonomy, one so pervasive that its occasional absence is more revealing than its constant presence.

The kyrios — the male guardian — was required for virtually every legal transaction a woman undertook in Greco-Roman Egypt.54 When Kronous, also called Philoumene, divorced her husband Lysimachos around 113-114 CE, she acted “with the assistance of her own father, Kastor, son of Pyrion.”55 When Thenstotoetis divorced her husband Lysas in 123 CE, she acted “with her kyrios her father Heron son of Neilos.”56 When Zois separated from Antipatros in the seventeenth year of Augustus’s reign, she acted “with her guardian, her brother Eirenaios son of Herakleides.”57 When Aurelia Thaesis gave her daughter in marriage in 260 CE, she acted “with Aurelius Theon also called Nepotianus.”58

The guardian was typically the woman’s father, brother, husband, or son — a male relative who cosigned her legal acts. The institution was Greek in origin. Egyptian law had long granted women broader autonomy — under Pharaonic and Ptolemaic demotic legal traditions, women could buy, sell, and litigate without a male intermediary.59 The imposition of the Greek guardianship system on Egypt was itself a form of colonial regulation, constraining women who had previously operated with greater independence.

But the guardianship requirement could also serve women’s interests. When Isidora daughter of Herakleides married Dionysios around 30 BCE-14 CE, the marriage contract stipulated that Dionysios must not “mistreat her or abuse her or throw her out or bring another woman into the house” — and if he did, Isidora’s kyrios, her brother Bakhios, had the right to enforce the penalty.60 The guardian was not merely a constraint on the woman; he was also a constraint on the husband. He was the family’s representative in a contract that bound both parties.

By the third century CE, the guardian requirement was weakening. The Constitutio Antoniniana of 212 CE, which extended Roman citizenship to virtually all free inhabitants of the empire, brought with it the ius trium liberorum — the right of women with three or more children to act without a guardian.61 A papyrus from Hermopolis dated to 304 CE records Demetria, also known as Ammonia, “acting without a guardian by the right of children.”62 The unnamed wife of Panetbeous, who had borne him children, may have possessed this right. It would have strengthened her legal position considerably.

Not all separations were bitter. The documentary record contains divorces so amicable that they read like business transactions — which, in a sense, they were.

Zois daughter of Herakleides and Antipatros son of Zenon separated from their marriage in the seventeenth year of Augustus (around 14 BCE-14 CE). The document records:

Zois acknowledges that she has received back from Antipatros, by hand from his house, the garments she had as her dowry portion, valued at 120 silver drachmae, and a pair of gold earrings. The marriage agreement is hereby void from this point. […] From this day, Zois shall be free to join herself to another husband, and Antipatros likewise to another wife, both being free from liability.63

The dowry returned. The marriage voided. Both parties free. The language is clinical, contractual, devoid of accusation. Neither party is blamed. Neither party claims ill-treatment. The marriage simply ended.

Kronous, also called Philoumene, divorced Lysimachos around 113-114 CE in an equally orderly fashion. She was twenty years old, he was thirty. They had a son named Herakleides, also called Valerius. The divorce document states that “each of them shall be free to manage his own affairs as he wishes, and Kronous to conclude a new marriage with the man whom she prefers” and that “none of the aforesaid persons shall proceed against one another in any way, neither on account of the possessions belonging to the marriage nor on account of any other matter.”64 Kastor, her father, confirmed receipt of the dowry. Lysimachos signed his assent. This was a negotiated settlement, witnessed and filed. The son was mentioned but not contested.

Thenstotoetis, about sixteen years old, divorced Lysas the freedman in 123 CE with similar procedural correctness. Heron, her father, received back the 300 drachmae dowry and “all the parapherna.” The document specifies that “it is possible for Thenstotoetis to join herself in marriage again, to whatever man she wishes, not being open to accusation from Lysas.”65 She was sixteen. She had been married under a contract drawn up two years earlier, when she was fourteen. She was now free to marry again. The document was witnessed by Marepsemis son of Pakebkis, “about forty-eight years old with a scar on his nose,” who wrote for both Lysas and the guarantor Sosas “since they do not know letters.” The sixteen-year-old girl, her forty-four-year-old father, and the twenty-two-year-old freedman ex-husband all agreed. The transaction was complete.

Thaesis, daughter of Thonis, divorced her husband Petosarapis in 96 CE at Oxyrhynchus. She acknowledged receipt of “the capital sum of 400 silver drachmae of the Imperial coinage which she brought to him with herself as her dowry,” along with the parapherna. The marriage contract was “returned to him cancelled in order to effect the dissolution of the marriage.”66 The physical destruction of the contract — crossing it out, cancelling it — was the material act that ended the legal relationship. Paper made the marriage. Paper unmade it.

These orderly dissolutions represent one end of the spectrum. At the other end: Serenus weeping, Syra petitioning for her squandered dowry, the unnamed wife of Panetbeous disappearing with the blanket and the pillow and the farm tools. Between the extremes lay the ordinary reality of most separations — negotiations conducted through intermediaries, property disputes settled by families, children parceled out according to custom and power.

sections.title: Reading Isidora Against the Grain
sections.body_html:
Return, then, to Serenus and Isidora, and read the letter with her in the room instead of only him.

She is married to Serenus. Perhaps he is her brother; perhaps not. They share a house. They bathe together. They have some form of domestic life.

Something goes wrong. Badly wrong. She leaves.

She communicates through an intermediary, and through him she levels the worst charge she can: Serenus made her a prostitute.67 In a world where marriages were defined and defended by paperwork, dowry, and household honor, the accusation says he did not merely mistreat her. It says he converted her into income.

She accuses him of selling her chain, probably the most valuable portable thing she owned.68 Then she accuses him of putting her in a boat, a phrase that still feels like the slam of a door and the shove away from shore.

She writes him letters. The letters are powerful enough to “shake a stone.” She is articulate. She is angry. She is not silent.

And then — as far as the documentary record tells us — she does not come back. Serenus asks: “Whether you are coming or not, let me know.” The question is plaintive, almost childish. It is the question of a man who has lost control of the situation. He can write. He can weep. He can refuse to bathe. But he cannot make her return.

sections.title: The State in the Marriage
sections.body_html:
Behind every private drama of marriage and separation stood the regulatory apparatus of the Roman state.

The Gnomon of the Idios Logos, the fiscal handbook that governed confiscations and penalties in Roman Egypt, devoted dozens of clauses to the regulation of marriage, inheritance, and sexual status.69 Its provisions reveal a state obsessively concerned with who married whom, what property changed hands, and what happened when marriages ended or never began.

The regulations on marriage age and childlessness were among the most intrusive. Clause 24: “A dowry given by a Roman woman over fifty years of age to a Roman man under sixty years of age is confiscated by the fiscus after death.” Clause 25: “Likewise also a dowry given by a woman under fifty to a man over sixty years of age is confiscated.” Clause 27: “Whatever a Roman, being sixty years old, childless, and unmarried, inherits from Romans, it is confiscated.” Clause 28: “If a woman is fifty years old, she does not inherit; but if she is younger and has three children, she inherits; if a freedwoman, if she has four children.” Clause 29: “A freeborn Roman woman having property of 20,000 sesterces, so long as she is unmarried, pays one hundredth per year.”70

These were applications of the Lex Iulia de maritandis ordinibus and Lex Papia Poppaea — the Augustan marriage legislation that penalized unmarried and childless Romans, confiscating portions of their estates and redirecting wealth toward those who had done their reproductive duty to the state.71 The legislation was designed to encourage Roman citizens to marry and produce children. In Egypt, it created an additional layer of fiscal regulation atop the existing Greek and Egyptian marriage customs.

The intermarriage regulations were even more complex. Clause 38: “Children born of a female citizen and an Egyptian remain Egyptians, but they inherit both parents.” Clause 39: “If a Roman or a Roman woman unknowingly cohabits with citizens or Egyptians, the children follow the inferior status.” Clause 45: detailed provisions for citizens who married Egyptian women, calibrated by the number of children. Clause 49: “It is not permitted for freedmen of Alexandrians to marry an Egyptian woman.” Clause 52: “It is permitted for Romans to marry an Egyptian woman.”72

The system was a matrix of status, ethnicity, gender, and fertility, with property confiscation as the enforcement mechanism. A woman’s legal identity — Roman, Alexandrian citizen, Egyptian — determined whom she could marry, what she could inherit, and what the state would confiscate from her estate. The private act of choosing a husband was embedded in a public system of classification and taxation. Love, in the Gnomon’s framework, was irrelevant. Status was everything.

sections.title: The Women We Can Still Name
sections.body_html:
Let us name the women this story has found in the papyri. They deserve to be listed, because the documentary record so often does not name them.

Isidora, who left Serenus and wrote letters that would shake a stone.

The unnamed wife of Panetbeous, who took the pillow and the blanket and the farm tools and went to live with Neilos.

Syra, daughter of Theon, who married a destitute man, received him into her parents’ house, conducted herself blamelessly, was beaten and starved, and petitioned the highest court in Egypt for her 200 drachmae.

Senyris, daughter of Har—, who was thrown out by a man who would not feed her children and who took another woman.

Makaria, whose husband threw her out and would not return her dowry.

Demetria the Koan, who married Herakleides the Temnitan in 310 BCE under a contract that protected her against his infidelity at double the cost of her own.

Isidora, daughter of Herakleides, who married her brother’s friend Dionysios around the turn of the era, bringing gold earrings and sixty drachmae, under a contract that forbade him from bringing another woman into the house.

Saraeus, daughter of Apion, who married the weaver Tryphon with a dowry of gold earrings and a milk-white robe.

Thaisarion, who brought to her marriage gold, silver, bronze, tin, a bathtub, five mantles, and ten and three-quarter arourae of land.

Aurelia Tausiris, whose mother gave her a pearl-studded dowry of turquoise veils and gold brooches.

Aurelia Aristous, daughter of a senator, who claimed two talents and 3,000 drachmae from her dead husband’s estate.

Kronous, also called Philoumene, who divorced at twenty and was free to “conclude a new marriage with the man whom she prefers.”

Thenstotoetis, who divorced at sixteen and was free to join herself “to whatever man she wishes.”

Zois, who separated from Antipatros with her garments and her gold earrings and no accusations.

Thaesis, who got her 400 drachmae back and had the marriage contract physically cancelled.

Euphemia, whose father dissolved her engagement because her fiance was “giving yourself over to lawless deeds.”

These women inhabited a legal system that simultaneously constrained them — requiring male guardians, penalizing unmarried women, confiscating estates that crossed status boundaries — and protected them — guaranteeing dowry return, permitting unilateral divorce, pricing husbandly infidelity at double the wife’s. They navigated a world of contracts and penalties, of gold earrings and agricultural tools, of letters that shook stones and petitions that vanished into bureaucratic silence.

Some of them won. Some of them lost. Most of them disappeared from the record entirely, leaving behind only their names in a marriage contract or a census return or a man’s angry petition.

sections.title: Epilogue: The Unanswered Question
sections.body_html:
Serenus’s letter ends with a question: “Whether you are coming or not, let me know.”

Isidora did not answer. Or she answered and the answer has not survived. Or she answered and he threw away her letter — as he seems to have thrown away his own.

The papyrus ended up in the rubbish of Oxyrhynchus, among thousands of other documents: tax receipts, loan contracts, property registrations, petitions, wills, census declarations, shopping lists, school exercises, literary fragments, and love letters both answered and unanswered. The dry climate of Upper Egypt preserved what the inhabitants discarded. The excavators retrieved what the sand had kept. The editors published what the excavators found.

And so Serenus survives, weeping by night and lamenting by day, unwashed for a month, asking a question that has waited nineteen hundred years for an answer.

Isidora’s silence is the answer. She left. She did not come back. She wrote letters that would shake a stone, and then she stopped writing — or she stopped writing to him. She may have married again. She may have returned to her family. She may have lived out her days in Oxyrhynchus or moved to another town. She may have been the wife of Panetbeous’s unnamed companion, or Neilos’s bride, or none of these — just one of the thousands of Egyptian women who lived and loved and left and were not recorded.

The papyri tell us what men wrote about women who defied them. They do not tell us what the women wrote to each other.

But Isidora wrote something. “Your letters,” Serenus says, “would have shaken a stone.”

We do not have the letters. We have only the stone.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 3
              Serenus to his beloved sister Isidora, many greetings. Before all else I pray for your health, and every day and evening I perform the act of veneration on your behalf to Thderis who loves you. I assure you that ever since you left me I have been in mourning, weeping by night and lamenting by day. Since we bathed together on Phaophi 12, I never bathed nor anointed myself until Athur 12. You sent me letters which would have shaken a stone, so much did your words move me. Instantly I answered you and gave the letter sealed (to the messenger) on the 12th, together with letters for you(?). Apart from your saying and writing “Colobus has made me a prostitute,” he (Colobus) said to me, “Your wife sent me a message saying ‘He himself (Serenus) has sold the chain and himself put me in the boat.’” You say this to prevent my being believed any longer with regard to my embarkation (?). See how many times I have sent to you! Whether you are coming or not, let me know. (Addressed) Deliver to Isidora from Serenus.

[...] of the Gnomon, which the deified Augustus established for the administration of the Idios Logos, and of the matters that have come under its authority either from emperors or the senate or the prefects from time to time or the idioi logoi — having abridged the main headings, I have set them out for you, so that by directing your attention to the conciseness of the written record you may easily have mastery of the matters.

1. Of those whose properties the fiscus takes over, their tombs were disregarded. But the deified Trajan, having learned that they simply deemed the tombs worthy of greater care for the enrichment of the fiscus and the creditors, granted them their burial monuments, but ordered the garden-tombs and such things to be sold; and being strict, he granted only debtors of the fiscus who were [bankrupt?] that their tombs remain as they are.

2. [...] it is not permitted for anyone except Romans alone to sell tombs free from financial encumbrance. For the deified Hadrian said that nothing among the Romans is free from financial encumbrance.

3. Of the revenue from properties initially submitted to the Idios Logos, one quarter is retained before [...].

4. Of those who die intestate and have no other heir according to the laws, their property is adjudicated to the fiscus.

5. Bequests made by Alexandrians to those who are not entitled pass to those who are able to inherit them according to the laws, provided they exist and lay claim.

6. It is not permitted for an Alexandrian to bequeath to a wife from whom he has no offspring more than one quarter of his estate; and if he has children by her, it is not permitted to allot to the wife more than the share he bequeaths to each of his sons.

7. Wills that are not executed through public transactions are invalid.

8. If a Roman will has the additional clause "and whatever I dispose of in accordance with Greek tablets, let it be valid," this is not admissible, for it is not permitted for a Roman to write a Greek will.

9. The freedmen of citizens who die childless and intestate are inherited by their patrons or their sons, if they exist and lay claim; but daughters or anyone else shall not inherit — rather, the fiscus.

10. Whatever a freedman bequeaths to someone who is not of the same status, it is confiscated.

11. A woman who is a Krenea does not inherit her child.

12. Children born of a Krenea woman and a foreigner inherit both their parents.

13. Children born of a female citizen and a foreigner become foreigners and do not inherit their mother.

14. It is not permitted for a citizen to bequeath to freedmen more than 500 drachmas or monthly payments of 5 drachmas.

15. It is not permitted for freedwomen of citizens to make wills, just as it is not for female citizens.

16. Whatever is bequeathed to freedmen of Romans with the provision that it pass also to their descendants — if it is shown that the descendants were not yet born when the bequest was written, upon the failure of the recipients, it is confiscated.

17. Bequests left for sacrifices for the deceased, when there are no longer those who will take care of them, are confiscated.

18. The inheritances made in trust from Greeks to Romans or from Romans to Greeks, the deified Vespasian confiscated; however, those who acknowledged the trusts received half.

19. Bequests made to freedmen who have not yet obtained legitimate manumission are confiscated. Legitimate manumission exists when the person being manumitted was over thirty years of age.

20. Bequests to a slave who had been in chains and was subsequently manumitted, or who was not yet thirty years old, are confiscated.

21. One manumitted under thirty years of age who obtains vindicta through the prefect is equal to one manumitted after thirty years.

22. Of Latins who die, their property is given to their patrons and their sons and daughters and heirs; bequests made by those who have not yet obtained legitimate freedom are confiscated by the Romans.

23. It is not permitted for Romans to marry their sisters or their aunts; marriage with brothers' daughters has been allowed. Pardalas, however, when siblings cohabited, confiscated their property.

24. A dowry given by a Roman woman over fifty years of age to a Roman man under sixty years of age is confiscated by the fiscus after death.

25. Likewise also a dowry given by a woman under fifty to a man over sixty years of age is confiscated.

26. And if a Latin woman over fifty gives something [...], it is likewise confiscated.

27. Whatever a Roman, being sixty years old, childless, and unmarried, inherits from Romans, it is confiscated. But if he has a wife and no children and registers himself, half is granted to him.

28. If a woman is fifty years old, she does not inherit; but if she is younger and has three children, she inherits; if a freedwoman, if she has four children.

29. A freeborn Roman woman having property of 20,000 sesterces, so long as she is unmarried, pays one hundredth per year; and a freedwoman having 20,000 sesterces pays the same, until she marries.

30. Inheritances left to Roman women having property of 50,000 sesterces who are unmarried and childless are confiscated.

31. It is permitted for a Roman woman to leave her husband one tenth of what she possesses; if more, it is confiscated.

32. Romans having more than 100 sesterces who are unmarried and childless do not inherit from Romans; those having less inherit.

33. It is not permitted for a Roman woman to dispose of property beyond the so-called coemptio; and a legacy left by a Roman woman to a minor Roman woman has also been confiscated.

34. Those in military service and those discharged from service are permitted to make wills both by Roman and Greek testaments and to use whatever terms they wish, each to bequeath to those of the same class and to those who are permitted.

35. Those serving in the military who die intestate — their children and relatives may inherit, whenever the claimants are of the same class.

36. Of those punished for murder or greater offenses or those who have voluntarily chosen exile for similar reasons, their property is confiscated; but one tenth is given to their children, and to their wives the dowries in money, and also one twelfth to themselves — Antoninus Caesar the lord granted this.

37. Those who have acted contrary to the ordinances of kings or prefects have been fined inconsistently: some one quarter of their estate, some one half, and some their entire property.

38. Children born of a female citizen and an Egyptian remain Egyptians, but they inherit both parents.

39. If a Roman or a Roman woman unknowingly cohabits with citizens or Egyptians, the children follow the inferior status.

40. Matters concerning those who introduce persons who should not be into the Alexandrian citizenship have now become a matter for the prefect's jurisdiction.

41. If an Egyptian picks up a child from the dung-heap and adopts it, after death a quarter is exacted.

42. Those who transact business under false designations are subject to the quarter-tax, and those who knowingly co-transacted with them are also subject to the quarter-tax.

43. When Egyptians wrote after the death of their father that their father was a Roman, a quarter was confiscated.

44. When an Egyptian registered a son as having come of age in the class of the two, a quarter is confiscated.

45. If a citizen marries an Egyptian woman and dies childless, the fiscus takes his acquired property; if he has children, it takes two thirds. If he had previously had children by a female citizen and has three or more children, the acquired property goes to them; if two, one quarter or one fifth; if one, one half.

46. Romans and citizens who unknowingly cohabited with Egyptian women were allowed to be free from penalty, and the children follow the father's status.

47. A female citizen who unknowingly cohabited with an Egyptian as if he were a citizen is free from penalty. But if the birth-declaration of children has been submitted by both, the citizenship is preserved for the children.

48. Citizens who marry women from the islands are treated the same as those who cohabit with Egyptians.

49. It is not permitted for freedmen of Alexandrians to marry an Egyptian woman.

50. When a freedwoman of a citizen had children by an Egyptian, Norbanus confiscated the property; but Rufus gave it to the children.

51. A son of a Syrian and a female citizen married an Egyptian woman and was condemned to the prescribed sum.

52. It is permitted for Romans to marry an Egyptian woman.

53. Egyptian women taken as wives by discharged soldiers, if they transact business as Roman women, are subject to the penalty for false designation.

54. Ursus did not permit a daughter of a discharged soldier who had become a Roman to inherit her mother who was an Egyptian.

55. If an Egyptian secretly enlists in a legion, upon discharge he is restored to the Egyptian status. Likewise those discharged from the rowing corps are restored, except only those from the fleet at Misenum.

56. Those serving in the military who do not have legitimate discharge, if they transact business as Romans, are subject to the quarter-tax.

57. Of the Paraetonioi who cohabit with women of another race or Egyptian women, the children follow the inferior status.

58. Those who have not registered themselves and those whom they should in the household census are subject to the quarter-tax; and if they have not registered in two censuses and are reported, they are condemned to double the quarter.

59. Romans and Alexandrians who have not registered those whom they should — whether one or more — are condemned to one quarter.

60. Those who have not registered slaves forfeit only the slaves.

61. The right to reclaim unregistered slaves is given to their masters, provided they have no other resources or only the slaves.

62. Those in military service who are unregistered are not penalized, but their wives and children are held liable.

63. Those charged with not having registered in the previous census — if the addition is within three years, they are pardoned.

64. Matters concerning those who sail out without a passport have now become a matter for the prefect's jurisdiction.

65. Slaves exported without the master's knowledge were sold.

66. Those who are permitted to sail but sail without a passport are subject to the one-third tax; if they export their own slaves without a passport, their entire property is confiscated.

67. Those who arrange for or sell household-born slaves of Egyptians from their household for the purpose of exporting them have been punished sometimes from their entire property, sometimes from half, sometimes from a quarter. Penalties have been fixed against those who connived; however, for the household-born slaves, even if they are not of Egyptian mothers, the maternal lineage is not examined.

68. A Roman who sailed out without having complete documents for departure was condemned to one talent.

69. An Egyptian woman who sent slaves through Pelusium with her sons [...] was condemned to 1 talent and 3,000 drachmas.

70. Those in public service are not permitted to purchase or lend in the districts where they conduct business, nor on their own accounts nor from surplus nor from public auction for an entire nome; those who become their agents are liable to the same penalty, and exchanges were sometimes held invalid. The penalties for such offenses are: if they buy from a private individual, a sum equal to the price of the goods purchased as a fine; if they lend, the same amount of principal, and the agents bear the same risk as they do; if the goods were sold, the price they received in good faith.

71. It is not permitted for priests to be engaged in any other occupation than the worship of the gods, nor to go about in woolen clothing, nor to wear their hair long, nor even if they are removed from the sacred [...].

72. It is not permitted to sacrifice unsealed calves; those who sacrifice contrary to this are condemned to 500 drachmas.

73. It is not permitted to borrow against priestly revenues.

74. A stolistēs who abandoned his religious duties was condemned to lose his revenues and a fine of 300 drachmas.

75. A priest who abandoned his religious duties was condemned to 200 drachmas; for wearing woolen clothing, 200 drachmas; a flute-player, 100 drachmas; a pastophoros, 100 drachmas.

76. A priest who wore woolen clothing and grew his hair was condemned to 4,000 drachmas.

77. Prophetships that pass by succession are reserved for the family.

78. Those that have been sold outright and not by preference are purchasable.

79. In every temple where there is a naos, there should be a prophet, and he receives one fifth of the revenues.

80. Stolistships are purchasable. Stolistai succeed the prophets. Only a prophet is permitted to wear the badge of justice. Pastophoroi are not permitted to transact business as priests. Pastophoroi are permitted to aspire to civilian ranks. Priestly privileges are preserved for the daughter. If a temple is short of personnel, it is permitted to take priests from a temple of equal rank for [...] village festivals. In Greek temples it is permitted for laymen to celebrate festivals. Inspectors of calves from notable temples are examined; prophets of secondary temples do not taste but are feather-bearers. Those who failed to send vestments for the apotheosis of Apis or Mnevis are condemned to a fine. Those who are removed from the sacred office on account of injury or incurable disease do not receive [...], but receive their maintenance allowances. The children born afterward to those who have been consecrated are not made priests. After the condemnation of priests, their sons are installed; but the sons born thereafter are made priests.

[...] It is not permitted for a [...] to serve as priest. Those who bury the sacred animals are not permitted to prophesy or to celebrate the temple festival or to tend sacred animals. Pastophoroi are not permitted to celebrate festivals or to claim priestly positions. Priests do not celebrate before feather-bearers. A priestly rank is not held by laymen.

Those who requested to make dedications and did make them were condemned to 500 drachmas on condition of making them. The maximum fine for forgery is 500 drachmas.

Those who have given written acknowledgments for a specified term through soldiers or such persons are not held liable. It has been prescribed that notaries register here in the city: for contracts from the Thebaid within 60 days, for the other nomes within 30 days, and for the city itself within 15 days; those who failed to register were condemned to 100 drachmas [...] and to register within the fifth of the following month. If any persons write a contract of mortgage or sale without authorization, they are condemned to 50 drachmas. If anything is lacking for the gymnasiarchs of the city for anointing, it is permitted to bring oil into the province and sell the surplus at the current price in the city, or else to forfeit the oil and pay a fine of 20 talents.

It is not permitted to lend on unharvested crops. It is not permitted to sell crops before they are gathered in, nor produce without inscription [...]. Of those who have lent at more than one drachma interest, half of their property is confiscated, and of the borrowers, one quarter.

It is not permitted to exchange coin for more than its value.

Of those picked up from the dung-heap, for males, after death, one quarter is confiscated.

Those who hold assemblies were condemned to 500 drachmas; sometimes only the presidents. It is not permitted for Caesariani to buy anything from an imperial estate. It is not permitted for vicarii to acquire anything or to marry free women. Soldiers were forbidden from acquiring property in the province in which they serve.

Of registered eunuchs and the impotent, after death, if they die intestate, the total is confiscated; if they make a will, two thirds [...]; but one third goes to whomever they may bequeath among those of the same class.

Those not found, if they register themselves, were released; if otherwise they are proven [...], they were condemned, and those who were recently unexamined — each of them is required to pay 500 drachmas for surety. Those before 5 years pay 20 talents [...] to the fiscus was confiscated [...] those who promise [...] promises [...] of the [...] [text lost].

sources.translation:
To king Ptolemy, greetings, Thamounis, Herakleopolitan. I am wronged by Thothortais, inhabitant of Oxyrhyncha in the Arsinoite nome. 
For in year 1, Hathyr, when I was visiting Oxyrhyncha and had gone into the baths, the accused, appearing on the scene and grabbing hold of me 
as I was bathing in a tub in the women’s room, cast me out of the tub. When I did not give way, she, disdaining me because I was a stranger, 
struck me a great deal on whatever part of my body she might hit, and took away from me my stone necklace. After this, when I called for help to 
Petosiris the kômarchês about these matters, Thothortais, after being summoned, addressed him when she chose; and the kômarchês, plotting with her, 
led me off to the phylakê and held me there for four days, before he stripped me of the himation which I was wearing, worth 30 drachmas, 
and which the accused now has. Thus was I released. I ask you, then, king, that you command Diophanes, stratêgos, to write to Moschion, epistatês, 
to send Thothortais to him and, these things being true, to force her to give me back my himation, the one worth 30 drachmas; and (that you command) 
Diophanes to pronounce judgment concerning the things that were done to me, so that through your agency, king, I may find justice. Farewell.
(in a second hand) To Moschion. By all means, reconcile them; but if you are unable, send them so that they may be judged before the laokritai. 
Year 1, Gorpiaios 28, Tybi 12.
               
            
            
               
                   Year 1, Gorpiaios 28, Tybi 12. Thamounis against Thothortais concerning a himation.
(J. Bauschatz, Law and Enforcement, pp242–243)

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 10
              For good fortune. Aurelia Thaësis daughter of Eudaemon and Heraïs, of Oxyrhynchus, acting with Aurelius Theon also called Nepotianus and however he is styled, has given her daughter Aurelia Tausiris in marriage to the husband Aurelius Arsinoiis son of Tryphon and Demetria, of the said city, to whom the said giver contributes as the dowry of her said daughter the bride in common gold on the Oxyrhynchite standard a necklace of the kind called maniaces, having a stone and weighing apart from the stone 13 quarters, a brooch (?) with 5 stones set in gold, weighing apart from the stones 4 quarters, a pair of ear-rings with 10 pearls weighing apart from the pearls 3 quarters, a small ring weighing 4 quarter, and in clothing at a valuation a silvery striped Dalmatian veil worth 260 drachmae, a white, single, tasselled, striped frock worth 160 drachmae, a turquoise-coloured Dalmatian veil worth 100 drachmae, another white Dalmatian veil with a purple border worth 100 drachmae, making the total of the whole dowry 1 mina 4 1/2 quarters of common gold, and for the valuation of the clothing 620 drachmae, a sum total to which no addition has been made; and questioned concerning the aforesaid dowry by the giver of the bride Aurelia Thaësis, the bridegroom Aurelius Arsinoüs agreed that he had received the full number at the aforesaid weight and valuation. Let husband and wife therefore live blamelessly together, observing the duties of marriage, and the husband shall supply his wife with al necessaries in proportion to his means; but if—which heaven forbid—in consequence of an estrangement a separation of the parties takes place, the husband shall restore to the giver of the bride, if she be living, or if not, to the bride, the aforesaid dowry in full within 60 days from the day on which a demand for restoration is made, the gold objects in accordance with the amount of the weight in each case. while, in respect of the clothes at a valuation, the bride’s representatives shall have the choice of keeping them at the valuation to be then made and receiving the balance in silver, or receiving the aforesaid valuation, the responsibility for the wear and loss of all these resting with the husband. If at the time of the separation the bride should be pregnant, the husband shall give her on account of the expenses of the birth 40 drachmae; and in connexion with the demand of the aforesaid dowry the bride’s representatives shall have the right of execution upon both the husband and all his property. This contract is valid,being written in duplicate so that each party may have one copy, and whenever they or one of them chooses, he shall make it public through the bureau, without requiring the concurrence of the other side or any further consent, because both sides now agree to the future publication, and to each other’s questions whether this is done rightly and fairly they have given their assent. The 7th year of the Emperors and Caesars Publius Licinius Valerianus and Publius Licinius Valerianus Gallienus Germanici Maximi Pii Felices and Publius Licinius Cornclius Saloninus Valerianus, the most noble Caesar, Augusti, Mecheir 2. (Signed) I, Aurelia Thaësis, have given my daughter in marriage to the above mentioned Arsinoüs and have made over to him the aforesaid dowry asaforesaid,and in answer to the formal question have declared my consent. I, Aurelius Theon also called Nepotianus, was associated with her and wrote on her behalf, as she is illiterate. I, Aurelius Arsinoüs, have received the aforesaid dowry, and if—which heaven forbid—a separation take place, I will restore it as aforesaid, and in answer to the formal question I have declared my consent.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 3
              The 11th year of the Emperor Caesar Trajanus Hadrianus Augustus, Pharmouthi 24, at Oxyrhynchus in the Thebaid, for good fortune, on the day of Julia Augusta, in the street. Sarapion son of Sarapion son of Sarapion son of Sarapion, his mother being Thais daughter of Sarapion, of Oxyrhynchus, has given in marriage his daughter Thais whose mother is ... to Sarapion son of Eudaemon son of Theon, his mother being Heras daughter of ... and Didous, who has received from Sarapion, the father and giver of the bride, a pair of ... weighing 3 minae 14 1/2 quarters, a brooch of 8 quarters, a ... of 6 quarters, a chain with 3 green ... of stone, the gold weighing [.] 1/2 quarters, making altogether on the standard of Oxyrhynchus 5 minae ... quarters, also 2 dresses, 2 girdles, one red the other rose-colored, a ... and a mantle, together worth 560 silver drachmae, and 1860 silver drachmae, the total value of the whole dowry being 4100 drachmae of silver of the Imperial coinage. Besides this the grandmother of the bride, Thais daughter of Sarapion and Heraclous, of the same city, with her guardian who is another son of hers and the full brother of the giver of the bride, Sarapion son of Sarapion, acknowledges in the same street that she has given away Thais in marriage, and she confers upon the said Thais (the possession of the slave) Callityche and her future offspring, the services of and the profits from her to be shared by the husband with the bride so long as they live together; and it shall not be lawful for the husband to ... the slave without his wife’s consent nor anything that is brought to him by his wife, nor to sell or mortgage or otherwise dispose of his property namely a house, yard and court and its fixtures and his slaves Sarapous and Nicarous and the children of Nicarous, Sarapous and Cerdon and Epicharmus, and the future offspring of them or ethers, or any additional property which he may acquire, without the consent of the bride. Let both live blamelessly together, and the husband shall supply the bride with necessaries in proportion to his means; but if any difference arises between them and the bride wishes to separate from her husband, as soon as the separation takes place the bride shall withdraw the slave Callityche and the children that may be born to her, and the husband shall repay to the giver of the bride if he survives, and if not, to the bride herself, the 4100 drachmae of the dowry within ... days from the day on which they are demanded or forfeit this amount increased by one half... And if the bride is at the time of separation in a state of pregnancy the husband shall give her on account of the birth 60 drachmae more. When they come together may they enjoy health; but if either husband or wife should chance to die, the husband shall have power over his own property to make any further provisions he pleases and to divide it among whom he will; but if he makes no further provisions the property shall after his death belong to their children. If the husband dies first the bride shall have... and she or her nearest relation on the one part and whoever shall be appointed by the husband on the other part shall together be guardians, the children being brought up with their mother until they come of age. If the husband appoints no guardian for the one part of the guardianship the bride or her nearest of kin shall act alone, and no one shall be permitted to deprive her of the guardianship nor any part of it. If the bride dies first without their having any children or when those that have been born have died childless, the husband shall repay the dowry namely ... the 4100 drachmae of silver in 60 days and shall send to the said relations of the bride all the rest of her property. Similarly if the husband dies first without their having any children or when those that have been born have died childless, the bride shall ... and withdraw the slave Callityche and the children that may be born to her, and until she has recovered them she shall have control over the whole property, and with regard to all the provisions the choice shall rest with the bride to have either if she prefers the aforesaid gold ornaments included in the dowry at the same weight or their equivalent value, and the bride and her agents shall have the right of execution upon both the husband and upon all his property in accordance with their agreement with each other. The certifier of both parties is Diogenes son of Hierax, scribe, of the same city, in the same street.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              To Heraclides, priest, chief justice, superintendent of the chrematistae and the other courts, from Syra, daughter of Theon. I married Sarapion, bringing him by cession a dowry amounting to 200 drachmae of silver. As he was destitute of means I received him into my parents’ house, and I for my part conducted myself blamelessly in all respects. But Sarapion, having squandered my dowry as he pleased, continually ill-treated and insulted me, using violence towards me, and depriving me of the necessaries of life; finally he deserted me leaving me in a state of destitution. I therefore beg you to order him to be brought before you, in order that he may be compelled perforce to pay back my dowry increased by half its amount. This petition is without prejudice to any other claims which I have or may have against him.

To Demophon, chief judge and superintendent of the
chrematistai and the other courts,  from Senyris,
daughter of Har[---], from the city of Oxyrhynchos in
the [Oxyrhynchite] nome.  I am being wronged by
N.N., son of Pankrates, from [the same (city). For,
although I was living] with him under a [contract of
maintenance], by whom [I’ve had] two children, [he
threw me out] and he doesn’t even feed or [clothe us.

 For these reasons [I made the complaint] against him

able (?) … Translation: Claytor, from the edition

Nor has he made restitution of the dowry. On the 19th of Pharmouthi of the present 26th year, the said husband, because I am without help, [...] delivered [...].

Translation: from P.Oxy. 1 ...eleventh indiction. I John, father of Euphemia, my unemancipated daughter, do send this present deed of separation and dissolution to you, Phoebammon, my most honourable son-in-law, by the hand of the most illustrious advocate Anastasius of this city of Oxyrhynchus. It is as follows. Forasmuch as it has come to my ears that you are giving yourself over to lawless deeds, which are pleasing to neither God nor man, and are not fit to be put into writing, I think it well that the engagement between you and her, my daughter Euphemia, should be dissolved, seeing that, as is aforesaid, I have heard that you are giving yourself over to lawless deeds and that I wish my daughter to lead a peaceful and quiet life. I therefore send you the present deed of dissolution of the engagement between you and her, my daughter Euphemia, by the hand of the most illustrious advocate aforesaid with my own signature, and I have taken a copy of this document, written by the hand of the most illustrious advocate aforesaid. Wherefore for the security of the said Euphemia my daughter I send you this deed of separation and dissolution written on the 11th day of the month Epeiph in the 11th indiction.
               + I, John, the aforesaid, father of Euphemia, my daughter, send the present deed of separation and dissolution to you, Phoebammon, my most honourable son-in-law, as is above written.

sources.translation: To Claudius Alexandros, centurion, from Panetbeous, public farmer from the village of Theadelphia. My cohabiting wife, from whom I also had children, having conceived hostile intentions toward our life together, took advantage of my absence and left my house some months ago without the so-called formal divorce, having carried off both her own things and very many of ours, among which was a large white undressed garment, an Oxyrhynchite pillow, a cross-striped double-blanket, materials for two tunics, and [...] and other agricultural tools. And although I sent to her many times, wishing to recover our property, she, being unrepentant, returned nothing -- despite the fact that I provided her with the maintenance allowance for the child. Since then I have now learned that a certain Neilos son of Syros from the same village has unlawfully seized and married her, I submit this petition and request that she and Neilos be brought before you, so that I may be able to obtain justice and recover our property, and I shall have been assisted. Farewell.

sources.translation: It was resolved by the council and the people of the Ptolemaians: when anyone sends away (his wife) or leaves (her), he shall register with the thesmophylakes his own name and that of his father, the month and the day. Let the wife also register in the same manner, and likewise the father, if he is still living. Let the hierothytai deposit the registrations [...] in the chest, or let them be liable. The dowry which [...] to the thesmophylakes with whom the registration has been made, he shall owe according to the law one and a half times, as in a judgment concerning dowry according to the law. Proof of those [...] dowries not yet [...] drachmas or less [...] If anyone does not return (the dowry) to the wife, let him pay [...] if he loses the case. Concerning a pregnant woman at the time of divorce: if the husband divorces her, let him wait. According to what has been agreed, let him support the wife, providing necessities for the mother; let him spend on the child when it is born whatever [...] to provide necessities for the child's mother [...] until the child sleeps [...] whenever he wishes, and to the wife [...]

Translation: Rowlandson, Women and Society (1998), Nr. 123
 In the reign of Alexander son of Alexander, in the seventh year, in the satrapship of Ptolemy, in the fourteenth year, in the month Dios. Marriage contract of Herakleides and Demetria. Herakleides (the Temnitan) takes as his lawful wife Demetria the Koan, a free man a free woman, from her father Leptines, Koan, and her mother Philotis, (Demetria) bringing clothing and ornaments to the value of 1000 drachmas, and Herakleides is to supply to Demetria all that is proper for a freeborn wife, and we shall live together wherever it seems best to Leptines and Herakleides consulting in common. If Demetria is discovered doing any evil to the shame of her husband Herakleides, she is to be deprived of all that she brought, but Herakleides shall prove whatever he alleges against Demetria before three men whom they both approve. It shall not be permitted for Herakleides to bring home another wife in insult of Demetria or to have children by another woman or to do evil against Demetria on any pretext. If Herakleides is discovered doing any of these things and Demetria proves it before three men whom they both approve, Herakleides shall give back to Demetria the dowry of 1000 drachmas which she brought and shall moreover forfeit 1000 drachmas of the silver coinage of Alexander. Demetria and those aiding Demetria to exact payment shall have the right to exact payment, as derived from a legally decided action, upon Herakleides himself and upon all Herakleides' property both on land and on water. This contract shall be normative in every respect, wherever Herakleides may produce it against Demetria, or Demetria and those aiding Demetria to exact payment may produce it against Herakleides, as if the agreement had been made in that place. Herakleides and Demetria shall have the right to keep the contracts severally in their own custody and to produce them against each other. Witnesses: Kleon, Gelan; Antikrates, Temnitan; Lysis, Temnitan; Dionysios, Temnitan; Aristomachos, Cyrenaean; Aristodikos, Koan.

Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              Tryphon, son of Dionysius, a Persian of the Epigone, to Saraeus, daughter of Apion, under the wardship of Onnophris, son of Antipater, greeting. I acknowledge the receipt from you at the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus through the bank of Sarapion, son of Kleandrus, of 40 silver drachmae of the Imperial and Ptolemaic coinage, and for the value of one pair of gold earrings, 20 drachmae of silver, and for a milk-white robe, 12 drachmae of silver, making a total sum of 72 drachmae of silver, to which nothing at all has been added, in consideration of which I have consented (to our marriage). And I will repay to you the
              72 drachmae of silver on the 30th of Phaophi in the coming second year of Gaius Caesar Germanicus Novus Augustus Imperator without any delay. If I do not repay in accordance with the above terms I will forfeit to you the said sum with the addition of half its amount, for which you are to have the right of execution upon me and upon all my property, as in accordance with a legal decision. If we separate from each other, you shall be empowered to have the pair of earrings at their present value. And since we are living together without a marriage contract, I further agree if as aforesaid owing to a quarrel we separate from each other while you are in a state of pregnancy, to ... so long as you ... This receipt is valid wherever and by whomsoever it is produced.
              There follow (1) the signature of Tryphon, written for him by Leon, (2) the signature of Onnophris, the guardian of Saraeus, written on his behalf by Theon, son of Paaeis, (3) the docket of the bank through which the payment was made, (4) the signature of Saraeus, written for her, in astonishingly badly spelled Greek, by Didymus, son of Boëthus, acknowledging that she had received back the sum mentioned in the agreement. This acknowledgement of Saraeus is dated Payni 15 in the 3rd year of Claudius.

‘Copy of agreement. The 13th year of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, the 22nd of the month Apellaeus, Phaophi 22, in Bacchias in the division of Heraclides of the Arsinoïte nome. Chaeremon son of Apollonius, a Persian of the Epigone, aged about 34 years, with a scar on the middle of the nose, acknowledges to Sisoïs son of Peteesis, aged about 71 years, with a scar on the left eyebrow, that he has received from him as dowry upon his daughter Thaisarion, who has formerly lived with Chaeremon as his wife, a hundred drachmae of silver coin, and as parapherna a pair of gold ear-rings weighing four quarters, a gold crescent of three quarters, two gold rings of two quarters, a pair of silver armlets to the weight of 44 drachmae of unstamped metal, two bracelets to the weight of 16 drachmae of unstamped metal; in raiment two robes, one white, one narcissus, and five mantles; and bronze utensils and a bath, to the total weight of four minae, and without weight two bronze … , and five minae of tin; and without valuation in usufruct as a gift from the present 13th year of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator the catoecic
holding of 10¾ arurae the property of Sisoïs in the area of Bacchias in two plots, consisting of 7¾ arurae of a holding in the place called Sade, the boundaries of which are, on the south the holding formerly belonging to Heraclides son of Zoilus, on the north the holding of Apollonius son of Straton, on the west the dyke called that of Archias by which the holding is drained, on the east the holding of Petheus son of Ptollis, separated by a common water-channel by which the holding is irrigated, and the second plot containing the remaining 3 arurae situate in the place called after Pieneouis, the boundaries of which are on the south the holding of Bacchion son of Ammonius, on the north the holding of Pa … eus son of … , on the west the dyke into which the holding drains, on the east the holding of Pisistratus. Let both Thaisarion and Chaeremon the parties to the marriage live blamelessly together as in their previous married life, Chaeremon carrying out all the yearly work of cultivation connected with the holding of 10¾ arurae presented, sowing and harvesting
the yearly produce and surplus produce resulting from them from the said present year, and carrying it to the home of their common wedded life, paying thereon all yearly public charges both of corn and money from the said present year. But if any difference arises between them and they separate the one from the other, either Chaeremon divorcing Thaisarion or Thaisarion withdrawing of her own free will, then the before-mentioned holding of 10¾ arurae shall belong to Thaisarion's father Sisoïs, or, if he be no longer living, to Thaisarion herself; and Chaeremon shall also restore the dowry aforesaid and the parapherna as they emerge from wear and tear, in the case of divorce forthwith, and in the case of voluntary withdrawal within 30 days from the day on which the demand is made to him. In what year soever the separation of the parties to the marriage takes place the proceeds of the holding for the twelve months of the year of separation shall be divided, and Chaeremon shall receive in proportion to the months during which Thaisarion shall have remained in the common house and Sisoïs the remainder, the public dues on the holding and seed-loans having first been deducted. As regards the terms of the contract Sisoïs, or, if he does not survive, Thaisarion and her assigns shall have the right of execution upon Chaeremon and all his property, as if in accordance with a legal decision. The subscriber of Sisoïs is … son of Hermas, aged about 41 years, with a scar on the right side of the forehead, Chaeremon being literate.’

Translation: from P.Oxy. 10
              Aurelia Aristous daughter of Aurelius Herodes son of Apion, ex-gymnasiarch, senator of the city of Oxyrhynchus, with the guardian given to me in accordance with Roman custom, Marcus Aurelius Nicocles son of Zoïlus, ex-gymnasiarch of the said city, to Aurelius Heraclides also called Lucius, son of Lucius, and however you are styled, greeting. In consequence of the lamentable news announced to me concerning the death of my blessed husband Achillion also called Apollonius, son of Apollonius, while at his post of basilicogrammateus of the territory of the Alexandrians, I agree that I have by this bond appointed you to go down to Alexandria and register with his excellency the procurator usiacus in my name on behalf of the son of myself and my said husband, and his heir, Aurelius Dionysius also called Apollonius, gymnasiarch of the said city, who is stil subject to the Laetorian law, all his (Achillion’s) property, valued at two hundred thousand sesterces. And out of this estate I declare that my husband owes me from the dowry which was brought to him upon my marriage with him consisting of gold ornaments and clothing and other objects valued at two talents and 3000 drachmae of silver ...
